title: SQL Statements
description: Core SQL statements for querying and manipulating database records.

sections.title: Column Data Types Guide
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

What you should be able to do after this lesson

  • Decide whether a piece of information should be a column, computed value, or a separate table.
  • Avoid common column design mistakes (e.g., full_name only, storing age).
  • Design searchable, consistent, and flexible columns using real-world examples.

sections.title: SQL Constraints
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Explain what SQL constraints are and why they matter.
  • Differentiate column-level and table-level constraints.
  • Use PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, CHECK, and DEFAULT correctly.
  • Apply referential actions (ON DELETE / ON UPDATE) to control relationship behavior.
  • Identify common constraint errors and fix them.

What Are SQL Constraints?

SQL constraints are rules enforced by the database to ensure the data stored is valid, consistent, and meaningful. Instead of relying on users or application code to behave correctly, constraints act like the database’s “automatic guardrails.”

Key idea: Constraints protect your database even if the application has bugs, or data is inserted manually.

Why Constraints Matter

  • Accuracy: prevents impossible values (e.g., negative quantity).
  • Consistency: ensures unique identifiers and non-empty required fields.
  • Referential integrity: stops “orphan” records (e.g., enrollment referencing a non-existent student).
  • Cleaner queries: less defensive filtering because data is already valid.

Where Constraints Can Be Written

Constraints can be defined in two main ways:

Column-level constraint

Written beside a column definition. Best for rules that apply to one column only.

Table-level constraint

Written after all columns. Best for multi-column rules (composite keys, combined checks).

CREATE TABLE Example (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,              -- column-level
  a  INT,
  b  INT,
  CONSTRAINT uq_a_b UNIQUE (a, b)   -- table-level (multi-column)
);

Core SQL Constraints (Most Common)

Constraint Purpose Typical Use Notes
NOT NULL Disallows missing (NULL) values Required fields (name, date, price) Does not prevent empty strings ('')
UNIQUE Prevents duplicate values Emails, usernames, student numbers Many DBs allow multiple NULLs in UNIQUE
PRIMARY KEY Uniquely identifies each row id columns, composite identifiers Implies UNIQUE + NOT NULL
FOREIGN KEY Enforces valid relationships Orders → Customers, Enrollments → Students Supports ON DELETE/UPDATE actions
CHECK Enforces a logical rule age >= 0, qty > 0, status IN (...) Support varies (older MySQL ignored it)
DEFAULT Assigns a value when none is provided created_at, is_active, country Does not apply if NULL is explicitly inserted
Reminder: Constraints enforce rules at the database level. Validation in the UI/app is still useful, but constraints are the final “truth enforcer.”

NOT NULL

NOT NULL ensures a column must always have a value.

CREATE TABLE Students (
  StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  FullName  VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  BirthDate DATE NOT NULL
);
Common confusion: NOT NULL does not stop empty strings like '' (for text). If you must prevent blanks, combine with CHECK where supported. 
-- Prevent empty names (works in many DBs that support CHECK)
FullName VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
CONSTRAINT ck_fullname_not_blank CHECK (TRIM(FullName) <> '')

UNIQUE

UNIQUE prevents duplicate values in a column (or set of columns).

CREATE TABLE Users (
  UserID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Email  VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE
);

Composite UNIQUE (multi-column): useful when a combination must be unique.

CREATE TABLE Sections (
  SectionID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  CourseCode VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  Term      VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,
  SectionNo INT NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT uq_course_term_section UNIQUE (CourseCode, Term, SectionNo)
);
Note on NULLs: Many databases allow multiple NULLs in a UNIQUE column because NULL means “unknown.” Behavior can differ by DB.

PRIMARY KEY

A PRIMARY KEY uniquely identifies a row. Each table should have one primary key. It can be single-column or composite.

Single-column Primary Key

CREATE TABLE Products (
  ProductID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  ProductName VARCHAR(120) NOT NULL
);

FOREIGN KEY (Referential Integrity)

A FOREIGN KEY ensures that a value in one table must exist in another table’s referenced key (usually a primary key). This prevents orphan records. 

CREATE TABLE Students (
  StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  FullName  VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE Enrollments (
  EnrollmentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  StudentID INT NOT NULL,
  SubjectCode VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT fk_enroll_student
    FOREIGN KEY (StudentID) REFERENCES Students(StudentID)
);

ON DELETE / ON UPDATE Actions

These actions control what happens to child rows when a parent row changes or is deleted.

Action Meaning Example Use
RESTRICT / NO ACTION Block delete/update if dependent rows exist Don’t delete a Student if enrollments exist
CASCADE Automatically delete/update dependent rows Delete all enrollment rows when student is deleted
SET NULL Set foreign key value to NULL Keep record but remove relationship
SET DEFAULT Set foreign key to its DEFAULT Move rows to “Unassigned” category 
CREATE TABLE Enrollments (
  EnrollmentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  StudentID INT,
  SubjectCode VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT fk_enroll_student
    FOREIGN KEY (StudentID) REFERENCES Students(StudentID)
    ON DELETE SET NULL
    ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
Be careful with CASCADE: It can delete lots of records quickly. Use it only when that behavior is truly intended.

CHECK

CHECK enforces a logical condition. If the condition is false, the insert/update is rejected. 

CREATE TABLE Payroll (
  EmployeeID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Salary DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT ck_salary_positive CHECK (Salary >= 0)
);

Common pattern: limiting allowed values (like status).

CREATE TABLE Tickets (
  TicketID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT ck_ticket_status CHECK (Status IN ('OPEN','IN_PROGRESS','RESOLVED','CLOSED'))
);
DB note: CHECK support differs by database/version. If your DB doesn’t enforce CHECK, you may need triggers or application validation.

DEFAULT

DEFAULT supplies a value automatically when a column is not provided in an INSERT statement. 

CREATE TABLE Accounts (
  AccountID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  IsActive  BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
  CreatedAt TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
Important: DEFAULT applies only when the column is omitted. If you explicitly insert NULL, DEFAULT usually won’t apply (unless combined with DB-specific features).

Naming Constraints (Best Practice)

You can (and should) name constraints for clearer error messages and easier maintenance. 

CREATE TABLE Users (
  UserID INT,
  Email  VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_users PRIMARY KEY (UserID),
  CONSTRAINT uq_users_email UNIQUE (Email)
);
Why naming helps: When an insert fails, the DB error often shows the constraint name—making it easier to identify the exact rule that was violated.

Adding, Dropping, and Modifying Constraints

Most DBs support ALTER TABLE to manage constraints after table creation.

-- Add a constraint
ALTER TABLE Users
ADD CONSTRAINT uq_users_username UNIQUE (Username);

-- Drop a constraint (syntax varies by DB)
ALTER TABLE Users
DROP CONSTRAINT uq_users_username;
Note: The exact DROP syntax differs (e.g., MySQL often uses DROP INDEX for UNIQUE). Always check your DB flavor.

Common Constraint Errors (and What They Mean)

Error Type Typical Cause Fix
NOT NULL violation Inserted NULL into required field Provide a value or allow NULL (if acceptable)
UNIQUE violation Duplicate value inserted Use a different value, or redesign uniqueness rule
PRIMARY KEY violation Duplicate key or missing key Ensure unique + not null key values
FOREIGN KEY violation Child references non-existent parent Insert parent first, or correct the FK value
CHECK violation Value breaks a logical rule Fix input data or adjust rule if wrong

Mini Demonstration Scenario

Below is a realistic academic mini-database using constraints to enforce clean data: students must have names, emails must be unique, and enrollments must reference existing students. 

CREATE TABLE Students (
  StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  FullName  VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  Email     VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  YearLevel INT NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT uq_students_email UNIQUE (Email),
  CONSTRAINT ck_students_yearlevel CHECK (YearLevel BETWEEN 1 AND 5)
);

CREATE TABLE Subjects (
  SubjectID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  SubjectCode VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  Title VARCHAR(120) NOT NULL,
  Units INT NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT ck_subjects_units CHECK (Units BETWEEN 1 AND 6)
);

CREATE TABLE Enrollments (
  StudentID INT NOT NULL,
  SubjectID INT NOT NULL,
  EnrollDate DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
  CONSTRAINT pk_enroll PRIMARY KEY (StudentID, SubjectID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_enroll_student FOREIGN KEY (StudentID)
    REFERENCES Students(StudentID)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT
    ON UPDATE CASCADE,
  CONSTRAINT fk_enroll_subject FOREIGN KEY (SubjectID)
    REFERENCES Subjects(SubjectID)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT
    ON UPDATE CASCADE
);

Hands-on Activity

Task: Improve a small “Library Borrowing” database using constraints.
  1. Create Members with:
    • MemberID as PRIMARY KEY
    • Email as UNIQUE + NOT NULL
    • FullName as NOT NULL
  2. Create Books with:
    • BookID as PRIMARY KEY
    • ISBN as UNIQUE
    • Status with CHECK (e.g., 'AVAILABLE', 'BORROWED')
  3. Create Borrowings with:
    • BorrowID as PRIMARY KEY
    • MemberID as FOREIGN KEY → Members(MemberID)
    • BookID as FOREIGN KEY → Books(BookID)
    • BorrowDate DEFAULT current date
    • ReturnDate CHECK (ReturnDate is NULL or ReturnDate >= BorrowDate)
Challenge upgrade: Add a composite UNIQUE constraint to prevent the same member from borrowing the same book multiple times without returning. (Example: UNIQUE(MemberID, BookID, BorrowDate) or a design you justify.)

Quick Self-Check Questions

  1. Why does PRIMARY KEY automatically imply NOT NULL and UNIQUE?
  2. What problem does FOREIGN KEY solve?
  3. When should you prefer a composite primary key over a single ID?
  4. Give a real example where ON DELETE CASCADE is appropriate, and one where it is dangerous.
  5. How is DEFAULT different from NOT NULL?

Key Takeaways

  • Constraints are database-level rules that protect data integrity.
  • Use NOT NULL for required fields, UNIQUE for non-duplicate fields, and CHECK for logical rules.
  • PRIMARY KEY identifies rows; FOREIGN KEY protects relationships.
  • Name constraints for easier debugging and maintenance.
  • Be intentional with ON DELETE/UPDATE actions—especially CASCADE.

sections.title: SELECT Statement
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Explain what the SELECT statement does and when it is used
  • Retrieve specific columns and all columns using *
  • Filter records using WHERE with operators and patterns
  • Sort results using ORDER BY and limit outputs using LIMIT
  • Use DISTINCT and basic aggregate functions with GROUP BY

Concept Overview

The SQL SELECT statement is used to read/retrieve data from one or more tables. Unlike INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE (which modify data), SELECT is mainly used to query and display results.

Key idea: SQL queries describe what you want, not step-by-step instructions on how to get it.

Sample Data

We will use a simple table named students for examples:

CREATE TABLE students (
  student_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  full_name  VARCHAR(100),
  course     VARCHAR(50),
  year_level INT,
  age        INT
);

INSERT INTO students (student_id, full_name, course, year_level, age) VALUES
(101, 'Maria Santos', 'BSIT', 1, 18),
(102, 'John Dela Cruz', 'BSCS', 2, 19),
(103, 'Ana Reyes', 'BSIT', 2, 20),
(104, 'Paolo Garcia', 'BSIS', 1, 18),
(105, 'Liza Mendoza', 'BSCS', 3, 21);

1) Basic SELECT

A. Select all columns

SELECT * FROM students;

* means “all columns”. This is useful for quick checking, but in real systems, selecting only the needed columns is better (faster + cleaner output).

B. Select specific columns

SELECT full_name, course FROM students;

2) Filtering with WHERE

Use WHERE to retrieve only the rows that match a condition.

A. Comparison operators

SELECT * 
FROM students
WHERE year_level = 2;
SELECT full_name, age
FROM students
WHERE age >= 20;

B. AND / OR / NOT

SELECT * 
FROM students
WHERE course = 'BSCS' AND year_level >= 2;
SELECT * 
FROM students
WHERE course = 'BSIT' OR course = 'BSIS';

C. IN (multiple matches)

SELECT * 
FROM students
WHERE course IN ('BSIT', 'BSCS');

D. BETWEEN (range)

SELECT * 
FROM students
WHERE age BETWEEN 18 AND 20;

E. LIKE (pattern matching)

SELECT * 
FROM students
WHERE full_name LIKE 'A%';
  • % = any number of characters
  • _ = exactly one character

3) DISTINCT (Unique values)

DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from the selected columns. 

SELECT DISTINCT course
FROM students;

4) Sorting and Limiting Results

A. ORDER BY

SELECT * 
FROM students
ORDER BY full_name ASC;
SELECT * 
FROM students
ORDER BY age DESC;

B. LIMIT (MySQL)

SELECT * 
FROM students
ORDER BY age DESC
LIMIT 3;
Tip: When using LIMIT, always combine it with ORDER BY if you want meaningful “top results”.

5) Aggregates and GROUP BY

Aggregate functions summarize data: COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MIN(), MAX().

A. COUNT all students

SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_students
FROM students;

B. Count students per course (GROUP BY)

SELECT course, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM students
GROUP BY course;

C. Filter grouped results (HAVING)

SELECT course, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM students
GROUP BY course
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2;
WHERE vs HAVING:
  • WHERE filters rows before grouping
  • HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Using = with NULL (use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL)
  • Forgetting quotes for strings (e.g., 'BSIT')
  • Using HAVING without grouping when you really need WHERE
  • Using SELECT * in reports when only a few columns are needed
-- NULL checks
SELECT * FROM students WHERE course IS NULL;
SELECT * FROM students WHERE course IS NOT NULL;

Quick Practice (Try It!)

  1. Display all students who are in BSCS.
  2. Show only full_name and year_level of students who are 2nd year.
  3. List students sorted by age from oldest to youngest.
  4. Show the top 2 oldest students.
  5. Count how many students are enrolled in each course.
Challenge: Modify a query so it returns only students whose name starts with “M”.

Hands-on Lab Activity (15–25 mins)

Scenario: You are building a “Student Lookup” feature for a registrar system.

Task A — Create the table and insert sample records

  • Create the students table (use the sample above).
  • Insert at least 8 students (mix courses and year levels).

Task B — Write queries for the lookup feature

  1. Show all students (all columns).
  2. Show only student_id, full_name, and course.
  3. Filter only 1st year students.
  4. Filter students where age is between 18 and 20.
  5. Search students whose name contains “del” (case depends on collation).
  6. Sort by course (A–Z), then by full_name (A–Z).
  7. Return only the first 5 results (use LIMIT).
  8. Count students per course and show only courses with 2 or more students.
Submission: Copy/paste your SQL queries and the result screenshots (or output table).

Summary

  • SELECT retrieves data from tables
  • WHERE filters rows, ORDER BY sorts results, LIMIT reduces output
  • DISTINCT removes duplicates
  • GROUP BY + aggregates summarize data, HAVING filters groups

sections.title: INSERT Statement
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Explain the purpose of the INSERT statement
  • Write INSERT queries for single-row and multi-row inserts
  • Insert values while controlling columns (including NULL and DEFAULT)
  • Use INSERT ... SELECT to copy data from one table to another
  • Identify common insert errors (data type, constraints, foreign keys)

What Is INSERT?

The INSERT statement is used to add new rows (records) into a table. Each inserted row must follow the table structure: correct columns, correct data types, and valid constraint rules (primary key, foreign key, NOT NULL, etc.).

Basic Syntax

The safest approach is to specify the column list explicitly. 


INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3)
VALUES (value1, value2, value3);
  • Columns and values must match in count and order.
  • Text values usually use single quotes: 'Maria'
  • Dates commonly use: 'YYYY-MM-DD'

Example Tables

Sample structure used in examples below


CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    full_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    course VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    year_level INT NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(120) UNIQUE
);

1) Insert a Single Row


INSERT INTO students (full_name, course, year_level, email)
VALUES ('Juan Dela Cruz', 'BSIT', 1, 'juan.delacruz@email.com');

Notice we did not insert student_id because it is AUTO_INCREMENT (MySQL will generate it automatically).

2) Insert Multiple Rows

You can insert many records in one statement (faster and cleaner). 


INSERT INTO students (full_name, course, year_level, email)
VALUES
('Maria Santos', 'BSCS', 2, 'maria.santos@email.com'),
('Paolo Reyes', 'BSIT', 1, 'paolo.reyes@email.com'),
('Anne Lopez', 'BSIS', 3, 'anne.lopez@email.com');

3) Insert With All Columns

Only use this when you are sure of the exact table column order. 


INSERT INTO students
VALUES (NULL, 'Kevin Tan', 'BSIT', 2, 'kevin.tan@email.com');
  • NULL here allows MySQL to auto-generate the student_id.
  • This style breaks easily if the table structure changes—prefer specifying columns.

4) NULL and DEFAULT Values

Use NULL when a column allows it. Use DEFAULT to use the column's default value. 


-- If email is optional (allows NULL)
INSERT INTO students (full_name, course, year_level, email)
VALUES ('Liza Cruz', 'BSCS', 1, NULL);

-- If a column has a default (example)
-- year_level INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 1
INSERT INTO students (full_name, course, year_level, email)
VALUES ('Noel Ramos', 'BSIT', DEFAULT, 'noel.ramos@email.com');

5) INSERT ... SELECT (Copy Data)

Instead of manually typing values, you can insert data coming from another table or query result. 


-- Example: copy 1st year BSIT students into a new table for orientation list
CREATE TABLE orientation_list (
    full_name VARCHAR(100),
    course VARCHAR(50)
);

INSERT INTO orientation_list (full_name, course)
SELECT full_name, course
FROM students
WHERE course = 'BSIT' AND year_level = 1;

Common INSERT Errors and Causes

Error / Problem Common Cause Fix
Column count doesn't match value count Number of values is different from number of columns Make sure columns and values align
Data truncated / incorrect type Wrong data type (text into INT, invalid date, etc.) Check column types and value formats
Duplicate entry Violates UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY constraint Use a unique value or change table rules
Cannot be NULL Trying to insert NULL into NOT NULL column Provide a value or update the table definition
Foreign key constraint fails Referenced record does not exist in parent table Insert parent record first, then child record

Good Practices

  • Always specify column names in INSERT statements
  • Insert parent rows first when using foreign keys
  • Validate formats (especially dates and numbers)
  • Use multi-row inserts for better performance
  • Test with SELECT after inserting to confirm results

Key Takeaways

  • INSERT adds new rows into a table
  • Columns and values must match in count and order
  • Multi-row insert reduces repeated statements
  • INSERT ... SELECT is useful for copying/query-based inserts
  • Constraints (PK/UK/FK/NOT NULL) control what inserts are allowed

Laboratory Objectives

  • Understand the purpose of inserting records into a table
  • Insert one record correctly into a table
  • Insert multiple records using one statement
  • Explain why multi-row insert is more efficient
  • Verify inserted data using SELECT

Given Table Structure

Create the table students with the following columns:

  • student_id (Auto Increment, Primary Key)
  • full_name (Required)
  • course (Required)
  • year_level (Required)
  • email (Unique, Optional)

Single Row Insert

Goal: Insert only one student record into the table.

Instructions
  1. Insert one new student into the students table.
  2. Provide values for:
    • full_name
    • course
    • year_level
    • email
  3. Do NOT manually insert a value for student_id.
  4. After inserting, verify your record using a SELECT statement.
Guide Questions:
  • Why did you not include the student_id?
  • What automatically happens to the primary key?

Multiple Row Insert

Goal: Insert multiple student records using a single INSERT statement.

Instructions
  1. Insert at least three (3) new students at the same time.
  2. Use only ONE INSERT statement.
  3. Each student must have:
    • Different full_name
    • Different email
  4. Do NOT insert student_id manually.
  5. Verify all inserted records using SELECT.
Performance Reflection: Why is inserting multiple rows in one statement better than writing three separate INSERT statements?

sections.title: UPDATE Statement
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Explain what the UPDATE statement does and when to use it.
  • Write safe UPDATE queries using WHERE conditions.
  • Update one or multiple columns in a single statement.
  • Use LIMIT and ORDER BY (MySQL) for controlled updates.
  • Apply best practices to avoid accidental mass updates.

Concept Overview

The UPDATE statement modifies existing rows in a table. Unlike INSERT (which adds new records), UPDATE changes values already stored. Because it can affect many rows at once, using a correct WHERE condition is critical.

Warning: Running UPDATE without WHERE updates all rows in the table.

Basic Syntax

UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = value1,
    column2 = value2,
    ...
WHERE condition;
  • table_name = the table you want to modify
  • SET = specifies which column(s) to change
  • WHERE = filters which row(s) should be updated

Examples

1) Update a Single Column

Scenario: Update the email of user with UserID = 101.

UPDATE Users
SET Email = 'maria.santos@email.com'
WHERE UserID = 101;

2) Update Multiple Columns

Scenario: Change both phone number and status for a specific user.

UPDATE Users
SET Phone = '0917-123-4567',
    Status = 'Active'
WHERE UserID = 101;

3) Update Multiple Rows Using a Condition

Scenario: Mark all students in section BSIT-2A as Enrolled.

UPDATE Students
SET EnrollmentStatus = 'Enrolled'
WHERE Section = 'BSIT-2A';

4) Update Using a Computation

Scenario: Increase all product prices in category 'Snacks' by 10%.

UPDATE Products
SET Price = Price * 1.10
WHERE Category = 'Snacks';

5) MySQL Controlled Updates (ORDER BY + LIMIT)

Scenario: Update only the latest 1 pending order (use with care).

UPDATE Orders
SET Status = 'Processing'
WHERE Status = 'Pending'
ORDER BY OrderDate DESC
LIMIT 1;
Note: ORDER BY and LIMIT in UPDATE are common in MySQL, but not supported the same way in all SQL databases.

Best Practices for Safe UPDATE

  1. Preview rows first using a matching SELECT. 
    SELECT *
FROM Users
WHERE UserID = 101;
  2. Use primary keys when possible (e.g., UserID) to target exactly one row.
  3. Update only the columns you need (avoid changing unrelated fields).
  4. Use transactions when doing risky updates (if your DB supports it). 
    START TRANSACTION;

UPDATE Products
SET Price = Price * 1.10
WHERE Category = 'Snacks';

-- If correct:
COMMIT;

-- If wrong:
-- ROLLBACK;
  5. Avoid mass updates accidentally — never forget WHERE unless you truly mean “all rows”.

Common Mistakes

❌ Mistake 1: Missing WHERE

UPDATE Students
SET EnrollmentStatus = 'Enrolled';

This updates every student.

❌ Mistake 2: Wrong Condition

UPDATE Users
SET Status = 'Inactive'
WHERE Status = 'Active';

This might be valid—but only if you truly intend to change all active users. Always confirm with a SELECT first.

❌ Mistake 3: Quoting Numbers Incorrectly (Context Matters)

Many databases allow quotes around numbers, but it can cause confusion. Keep types consistent with your schema (INTEGER vs VARCHAR).

Quick Check (Try This)

  1. Write an UPDATE query that changes a student’s Section to BSIT-2B for StudentID = 20230012.
  2. Write an UPDATE query that increases all prices by 5% for products where Stock < 10.
  3. Why is it safer to run a SELECT first before an UPDATE?

Hands-on Lab Activity: Safe Updates in a Student Enrollment Table

Goal: Practice updating records using a realistic table and verifying changes before committing them.

Step 1: Create the Table

CREATE TABLE Students (
    StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    FullName VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Section VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    EnrollmentStatus VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'Pending'
);

Step 2: Insert Sample Data

INSERT INTO Students (StudentID, FullName, Section, EnrollmentStatus) VALUES
(20230001, 'Maria Santos', 'BSIT-2A', 'Pending'),
(20230002, 'Juan Dela Cruz', 'BSIT-2A', 'Pending'),
(20230003, 'Ana Reyes', 'BSIT-2B', 'Enrolled'),
(20230004, 'Paolo Lim', 'BSIT-2A', 'Pending');

Step 3: Preview Before Updating

SELECT * FROM Students
WHERE Section = 'BSIT-2A' AND EnrollmentStatus = 'Pending';

Step 4: Perform the UPDATE

UPDATE Students
SET EnrollmentStatus = 'Enrolled'
WHERE Section = 'BSIT-2A' AND EnrollmentStatus = 'Pending';

Step 5: Verify the Result

SELECT * FROM Students
ORDER BY StudentID;

Lab Questions

  1. How many rows were updated? (Check the affected rows message)
  2. Which part of the query ensured only BSIT-2A pending students were updated?
  3. What would happen if the WHERE clause was removed?

Summary

  • UPDATE modifies existing records (not adding new ones).
  • WHERE is the safety filter—use it to target specific rows.
  • Preview with SELECT and verify after updating.
  • For risky updates, use transactions (COMMIT/ROLLBACK).
Prepared for SQL Fundamentals • Practice safe updates ✅

sections.title: DELETE Statement
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Explain what DELETE does and how it differs from TRUNCATE and DROP.
  • Write DELETE statements with WHERE conditions to remove specific rows.
  • Apply safety checks (preview with SELECT, transactions, backups) before deleting.
  • Understand how foreign keys and cascading rules affect delete operations.
  • Use deletion best practices, including “soft delete” when appropriate.

Concept: What Does DELETE Do?

The SQL DELETE statement removes rows from a table. It does not remove the table itself—only the records that match your condition.

Warning: If you run DELETE without a WHERE clause, it can remove all rows in the table.

DELETE vs TRUNCATE vs DROP

Command Removes Can use WHERE? Table remains?
DELETE Selected rows ✅ Yes ✅ Yes
TRUNCATE All rows (fast) ❌ No ✅ Yes
DROP Table structure + all data ❌ No ❌ No

Basic Syntax

DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE condition;
Best Practice: Always write the WHERE first in your mind: “What exactly am I deleting?”

Scenario: Small Online Shop

You manage a small online shop database. Some records must be removed: cancelled orders, outdated temporary data, or duplicate entries.

Sample Tables

-- Customers table
CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  full_name   VARCHAR(100),
  email       VARCHAR(120) UNIQUE
);

-- Orders table (linked to customers)
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id INT,
  status      VARCHAR(20),
  order_date  DATE,
  FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
);

-- Example: add a "soft delete" flag (optional pattern)
-- ALTER TABLE customers ADD COLUMN is_deleted TINYINT(1) DEFAULT 0;

Examples

1) Delete a specific row by primary key

DELETE FROM customers
WHERE customer_id = 101;

2) Delete rows that match a condition

DELETE FROM orders
WHERE status = 'CANCELLED';

3) Delete within a date range

DELETE FROM orders
WHERE order_date < '2024-01-01'
  AND status = 'CANCELLED';

4) Preview first (recommended)

Before deleting, run the same condition using SELECT:

SELECT *
FROM orders
WHERE order_date < '2024-01-01'
  AND status = 'CANCELLED';

5) Safe delete pattern with LIMIT (MySQL)

Use LIMIT when you expect only a small number of rows, so you don’t accidentally wipe many rows. 
DELETE FROM orders
WHERE status = 'CANCELLED'
LIMIT 5;

6) Delete using JOIN (MySQL syntax)

Delete cancelled orders of a specific customer email:

DELETE o
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE c.email = 'maria.santos@email.com'
  AND o.status = 'CANCELLED';

Foreign Keys and Cascading Deletes

When tables are related (parent-child), deleting a parent row may fail if child rows still exist (to protect data integrity). This depends on the foreign key rule.

Common outcomes

  • RESTRICT / NO ACTION: You can’t delete the parent if children exist.
  • CASCADE: Deleting the parent automatically deletes matching child rows.
  • SET NULL: Child foreign key is set to NULL when the parent is deleted.
-- Example: cascade (be careful!)
CREATE TABLE order_items (
  item_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
  order_id INT,
  product  VARCHAR(80),
  qty      INT,
  FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES orders(order_id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE
);
Reminder: ON DELETE CASCADE is powerful—one delete can remove many dependent rows.

Safety Checklist (Use This Every Time)

  1. Preview: Run a SELECT with the same WHERE.
  2. Count rows: SELECT COUNT(*) to confirm expected affected rows.
  3. Transaction (if supported): Delete then ROLLBACK if wrong.
  4. Limit small deletes: Use LIMIT when applicable.
  5. Backups: Know how to restore (export / dump) before mass deletes.
-- Transaction example (InnoDB)
START TRANSACTION;

DELETE FROM orders
WHERE status = 'CANCELLED'
  AND order_date < '2024-01-01';

-- Check affected rows quickly (or preview before running DELETE)
-- If correct:
COMMIT;

-- If wrong:
-- ROLLBACK;

Soft Delete (When You Should Consider It)

Instead of permanently deleting, many systems mark records as “deleted” so they can be restored or audited later. 

-- Soft delete pattern
UPDATE customers
SET is_deleted = 1
WHERE customer_id = 101;

-- Query active customers only
SELECT *
FROM customers
WHERE is_deleted = 0;
Soft delete is useful for audit trails, recoverability, and compliance. Hard delete is useful for removing truly invalid/test data or meeting data retention policies.

Hands-on Lab Activity: “Delete Safely” Practice

Goal: Practice deleting the correct rows while avoiding accidental mass deletion.

Task A: Setup

CREATE DATABASE shop_db;
USE shop_db;

CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  full_name   VARCHAR(100),
  email       VARCHAR(120) UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id INT,
  status      VARCHAR(20),
  order_date  DATE,
  FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
);

INSERT INTO customers VALUES
(101,'Maria Santos','maria@email.com'),
(102,'Juan Dela Cruz','juan@email.com'),
(103,'Ana Reyes','ana@email.com');

INSERT INTO orders VALUES
(1,101,'CANCELLED','2023-10-01'),
(2,101,'PAID','2023-10-10'),
(3,102,'CANCELLED','2022-05-12'),
(4,103,'CANCELLED','2024-02-01'),
(5,103,'PAID','2024-02-05');

Task B: Required Deletes (Answer with SQL Commands)

  1. Delete only the order with order_id = 3.
  2. Delete all CANCELLED orders before 2024-01-01.
  3. Delete cancelled orders of customer 101 only (do not delete paid orders).

Task C: Verification

After each delete, prove your result by running:

SELECT * FROM orders ORDER BY order_id;

Challenge (Optional)

  • Add LIMIT to a delete and explain why it increases safety.
  • Try deleting customer 101. What error happens? Explain why (foreign key constraint).

Quick Summary

  • DELETE removes rows; use WHERE to target specific records.
  • Preview with SELECT first to avoid mistakes.
  • Foreign keys may block deletes unless rules allow it (e.g., CASCADE).
  • For recoverability, consider soft delete (flagging instead of removing).

sections.title: SQL JOINs
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Explain why JOINs are needed in relational databases.
  • Differentiate INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL joins.
  • Write JOIN queries using correct ON conditions and table aliases.
  • Avoid common JOIN mistakes (wrong keys, duplicated rows, filtering in the wrong place).

Prerequisites

  • Basic SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY
  • Understanding of primary key (PK) and foreign key (FK)

Core Idea

A JOIN lets you retrieve related data stored across multiple normalized tables. Instead of repeating information in one big table, we connect tables through matching keys (usually PK ↔ FK).

Rule of thumb
JOIN chooses which rows match (via ON) and WHERE applies filters after joining (unless you intentionally filter before with a subquery).

Sample Schema (Used in Examples)

We will use a simple ordering system:

Table Key Columns Purpose
customers customer_id (PK) Stores customer info
orders order_id (PK), customer_id (FK) Stores orders made by customers
order_items order_item_id (PK), order_id (FK), product_id (FK) Stores items inside an order
products product_id (PK) Stores product info

Quick relationship map:

  • customers (1) → (many) orders
  • orders (1) → (many) order_items
  • products (1) → (many) order_items

Basic JOIN Syntax

SELECT
  ...
FROM tableA AS a
JOIN tableB AS b
  ON a.key = b.key;
Aliases matter
Use short aliases (c, o, oi, p) to keep queries readable, especially with 3+ tables.

1) INNER JOIN

Returns only rows that have matching values in both tables. If there is no match, the row is excluded. 

-- List orders with the customer name
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  o.order_date,
  c.customer_name
FROM orders AS o
INNER JOIN customers AS c
  ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
ORDER BY o.order_date DESC;
When to use
When you only want records that definitely have a related match (e.g., orders must have a customer).

2) LEFT JOIN (LEFT OUTER JOIN)

Returns all rows from the left table, and matching rows from the right table. If there is no match, right-side columns become NULL. 

-- Show all customers and any orders they may have (including customers with no orders)
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  o.order_id,
  o.order_date
FROM customers AS c
LEFT JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
ORDER BY c.customer_name;
Common use
Finding “missing” related data (e.g., customers with no orders).
-- Customers with NO orders (classic LEFT JOIN + NULL check)
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name
FROM customers AS c
LEFT JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_id IS NULL;

3) RIGHT JOIN (RIGHT OUTER JOIN)

Returns all rows from the right table, and matching rows from the left table. In practice, many developers prefer rewriting this as a LEFT JOIN (for consistency). 

-- Example (same result can usually be written as a LEFT JOIN by swapping tables)
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  o.order_id
FROM customers AS c
RIGHT JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;

4) FULL OUTER JOIN

Returns all rows from both tables. Matches are combined; non-matches appear with NULL on the missing side.

MySQL note
MySQL does not support FULL OUTER JOIN directly. You can simulate it using LEFT JOIN + UNION with a RIGHT JOIN. 
-- FULL OUTER JOIN simulation in MySQL
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  o.order_id,
  o.order_date
FROM customers AS c
LEFT JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id

UNION

SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  o.order_id,
  o.order_date
FROM customers AS c
RIGHT JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;

JOINing 3+ Tables (Most Real Queries)

When you join multiple tables, connect each table using the correct relationship path. (Avoid “guessing” keys.) 

-- Show order details with product names and line totals
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  o.order_date,
  c.customer_name,
  p.product_name,
  oi.quantity,
  oi.unit_price,
  (oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS line_total
FROM orders AS o
INNER JOIN customers AS c
  ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
INNER JOIN order_items AS oi
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
INNER JOIN products AS p
  ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
ORDER BY o.order_id;
Why duplicates happen
If one order has many items, the order row will appear many times—one per item. That’s expected in detail reports.

Aggregation with JOIN (Totals per Customer / Order)

-- Total sales per order
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS order_total
FROM orders AS o
INNER JOIN order_items AS oi
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY o.order_id
ORDER BY order_total DESC;
-- Total sales per customer (customers with no orders will not appear)
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_spent
FROM customers AS c
INNER JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
INNER JOIN order_items AS oi
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;
-- Total sales per customer (including customers with no orders -> shows NULL, use COALESCE to show 0)
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  COALESCE(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 0) AS total_spent
FROM customers AS c
LEFT JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
LEFT JOIN order_items AS oi
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

Common JOIN Mistakes (and Fixes)

Mistake What happens Fix
Joining on the wrong columns Wrong matches / huge result sets Follow PK ↔ FK relationships; verify keys
Forgetting the ON condition Cross join (cartesian product) explosion Always specify correct ON
Filtering LEFT JOIN results in WHERE incorrectly LEFT JOIN behaves like INNER JOIN Move right-table filter into ON when needed
Selecting * with many tables Confusing columns / duplicates Select only needed columns with aliases 
-- Example: keeping LEFT JOIN behavior while filtering orders by year
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  o.order_id,
  o.order_date
FROM customers AS c
LEFT JOIN orders AS o
  ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
  AND YEAR(o.order_date) = 2026;  -- filter placed in ON, not WHERE

Hands-on Lab Activity (JOIN Practice)

Goal: Write JOIN queries using the schema above.

  1. INNER JOIN: Display each order with the customer name.
    Output columns: order_id, order_date, customer_name
  2. LEFT JOIN: List all customers, including those without orders.
    Output columns: customer_id, customer_name, order_id
  3. 3-table JOIN: Show order line items with product name.
    Output columns: order_id, product_name, quantity, unit_price, line_total
  4. Aggregation: Compute total amount per order and sort highest to lowest.
    Output columns: order_id, order_total
  5. Challenge: Find customers with no orders.
    Output columns: customer_id, customer_name

Quick Check (Exit Questions)

  1. What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?
  2. Why does an order appear multiple times when joining with order_items?
  3. When should you filter in the ON clause instead of WHERE?

Summary

  • INNER JOIN = only matching rows.
  • LEFT JOIN = keep all left rows, unmatched right becomes NULL.
  • Multi-table JOINs follow relationships (PK ↔ FK) step-by-step.
  • Aggregation + JOIN is used for totals and reports.
